The probability of any organized armed conflict emerging is extremely low, but slightly higher uncertainty for the more distant date (2026-02-07) accounts for the remote possibility of low-probability geopolitical shifts.

Based on FRED data analysis: The index shows a downward trend from 5.35% in Jan 2025 to 4.84% on 2025-11-07, though with recent volatility. The recent uptick from 4.67% to 4.84% suggests some upward momentum, but the overall 2025 trend has been downward, creating balanced uncertainty.

Based on a combination of statistical trend analysis from FRED data (which initially suggested ~0.61 probability of increase), Fed policy context showing clear downward pressure from rate cuts, and economic fundamentals: The Fed has delivered 50bp of cuts (Sept and Oct 2025) and is signaling one more 25bp cut expected by year-end per SEP dots. The 1-year T-bill rate was 3.49% on 2025-11-07 (last trading day before the 2025-11-09 baseline weekend date). Recent momentum has been downward. Economic data shows slowing growth (GDP 1.6% in H1 2025 vs 2.6% in H2 2024) and inflation moving toward 2% target, supporting continued easing. For 2025-11-16 (7 days out): 0.38 - near-term still has some uncertainty but downward pressure dominates. For 2025-12-09 (30 days out): 0.35 - additional time allows for more Fed easing signals and continued rate cut expectations to filter through. For 2026-02-07 (90 days out): 0.42 - increased uncertainty at longer horizon, but still below 0.50 as Fed easing cycle likely continues into early 2026. The upward adjustment from Dec to Feb reflects: (a) increased uncertainty at longer horizons (as required), and (b) possibility that by Feb 2026 the market might start pricing in the end of the easing cycle or eventual reversal if economic conditions stabilize.
